skill.name: nano-banana-builder
skill.description: Build Next.js web applications that use Google Gemini Nano Banana image generation APIs for text-to-image, editing, galleries, storage, and rate limiting.
skill.summary: Create production-oriented Gemini image generation apps with Next.js, AI SDK, storage, and quota patterns.
skill.author: CesarAugustusGroB

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and blocker patterns, but review found they are mostly Markdown code fences, inline model names, placeholder environment examples, and normal API sample code. The skill does not appear malicious, but it teaches networked image generation with environment credentials and public blob storage, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Public Storage Pattern for Generated Images and Prompts
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends object storage examples that use public access for generated images and add prompt text as metadata. This is legitimate for public galleries, but copied code could expose user images or sensitive prompt content without an access control review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly show public blob storage and prompt metadata in sample code. The risk is contextual because the code is documentation, not an active payload.

medium_findings.title: Networked Generation and Editing Examples Use User Content
medium_findings.description: The examples send prompts, image data, and edit history to API routes or Gemini model calls. This is expected for an image generation builder, but implementers must treat prompts and uploaded images as user data and apply privacy controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced examples clearly make model or API calls with user-supplied prompts and image content. This is normal functionality, but it creates real data-handling obligations.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Require Server-Side Handling
low_findings.description: The skill documents API keys and storage tokens in environment variables. This is the correct pattern when kept server-side, but copied applications must avoid exposing these values in client bundles or logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are explicit placeholder environment configuration, not real secrets. The residual risk is implementation error by downstream users.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many backticks as command execution. Review found these are Markdown code fences, inline model names, npm install examples, TypeScript snippets, and documentation text, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited regions are fenced Markdown examples and inline documentation. No evidence found that the skill itself executes shell commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Keylogger Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography findings map to words such as image generation model names, hash-oriented cache examples, and status-code checks. The keylogger finding maps to an anti-pattern warning against generating on every keystroke.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text does not implement cryptography or keylogging. It describes model selection, caching strategy, and a warning not to trigger generation on each keystroke.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public Blob Access in Sample Storage Code
dangerous_patterns.description: Sample code stores generated images with public blob access. Users should change this pattern when generated assets or prompts are private.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sample explicitly sets public access. The pattern is not malicious but can be unsafe for private user content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Client-Supplied Image Data Sent to API Route
dangerous_patterns.description: The iterative editor example sends base64 image data, an edit prompt, and history to an API endpoint. Applications should authenticate requests and limit payload size.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code shows client content being posted to an API route. The risk depends on the final application controls around authentication and validation.

content.value_statement: Building image generation apps requires correct model names, storage choices, and quota handling. This skill provides practical Next.js and AI SDK patterns for Nano Banana apps.
actual_capabilities: Selects the correct Gemini image generation model for speed or quality needs.
Outlines Next.js server actions and API routes for image generation workflows.
Provides React component patterns for generators, galleries, and iterative editors.
Documents storage options using Vercel Blob, S3, or R2-compatible services.
Explains rate limiting, retry handling, and cost-control patterns for production apps.
Covers prompt, aspect ratio, and response modality choices for image outputs.
limitations: Does not provide a complete deployable application by itself.
Requires valid Google Gemini API access and related service credentials.
Examples need security review before handling private user images or prompts.
Model availability, pricing, and quota details may change after publication.

use_cases.title: Prototype an Image Generator
use_cases.description: Create a Next.js text-to-image tool with model selection, loading states, and generated image previews.

use_cases.title: Add Iterative Image Editing
use_cases.description: Build a workflow where users revise generated images through conversational edit prompts and history.

use_cases.title: Plan Production Operations
use_cases.description: Choose storage, rate limits, retries, and cost controls before launching a public generation app.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Generator
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple text-to-image app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nano-banana-builder to create a basic Next.js image generator with a prompt input, model selection, and loading state.

prompt_templates.title: Add Storage and Gallery
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate feature work for saved outputs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nano-banana-builder to add Vercel Blob or S3 storage and a gallery view for generated images.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Iterative Editor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nano-banana-builder to design an iterative image editor that keeps conversation history and supports multi-turn edits.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for Production Launch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use nano-banana-builder to review my image generation app for model choice, rate limits, storage privacy, retries, and cost controls.

output_examples.input: I need a product mockup generator for marketing images.
output_examples.output: A recommended app structure with a prompt form, model selector, server-side generation flow, object storage plan, and gallery behavior.

output_examples.input: I want users to edit one generated image many times.
output_examples.output: An iterative editing workflow that tracks the current image, user edit prompts, generation history, and loading states.

output_examples.input: I am launching publicly and need cost controls.
output_examples.output: A production checklist covering rate limits, retry behavior, caching, storage choices, and user-facing error messages.

best_practices: Keep API keys and storage tokens on the server side only.
Use fast models for drafts and higher quality models for final images.
Store generated images with access controls that match the user privacy model.
anti_patterns: Do not expose Gemini or storage credentials in client-side code.
Do not store large base64 images directly in an application database.
Do not generate images on every keystroke or without quota controls.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Next.js apps that generate, edit, store, and display images using Gemini Nano Banana models.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include production guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers storage, rate limits, retries, cost controls, and common error handling patterns.

faq.question: Does it provide real API keys?
faq.answer: No. It only shows placeholder environment variable names for users to configure themselves.

faq.question: Can it handle private user images safely by default?
faq.answer: No. The examples need access control and privacy review before handling private images or prompts.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, but it teaches network calls, environment credentials, and public storage patterns that require care.
